speaker: interviewer
text: Abelesz. My name is Sharon Tyler. We're in London, and this is tape three. You were just telling me about knowing that the-- that the gypsies going, that sort of your-- your fate was sealed, and telling me about your experience in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said before, Auschwitz-- I was in a barrack which was for youngsters. Those-- those who came into the camp, not much-- not many younger than me, perhaps about the same age. But they were all taller. Between the age of, I would say, from 14 to 16. A lot of-- a lot of boys from all over-- all over Hungary. We tried to group ourselves among localities because there was so much difference between the people from different-- different part of Hungary. Some-- most of the people from eastern Hungary, they speak Yiddish. In western Hungary, we spoke Hungarian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also there, then we met the Polish prisoners who were already longer. And they-- they were more wiser than us, more experienced. And we learned a lot from them about how to try to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you learn from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: First off, you have to-- you have to be avoid-- to try to be physically as fit as possible. And that's only way you can-- you can do it, if you to get some extra food from whatever the ration they giving you. Secondly, always avoid whenever there is selection. At that time, we knew that there is going to be some selections. It was obvious because from the camps, they were taking people to-- to work. So when people were taken to work, they were-- they were-- they looked at it, and people who looked like they were capable to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people who were older, or looked sick, or-- or youngsters, they were always left behind. When I looked around my-- towards the end of the summer, the-- the whole camp was only people who are-- who are-- how should I say it? Not capable to work. Leftovers, like me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I was-- I don't-- I was 14-year-old, but I was small and-- and quite thin. And so how I got it into the camp was just by the good luck of Mengele had-- had a whim. And I was worried about the future. My brother could have-- could have been gone away, but he always-- to-- from Auschwitz. I didn't want to stay in Auschwitz because those-- those chimneys were constantly burning. And-- and-- and I knew what's going on a few hundred yards from us. And-- but I was never taken into-- away into-- into transport. So when it came to selection, it started in the autumn. Immediately, the first selection, which was Erev Rosh Hashanah, the day before Rosh Hashanah. And-- and I was selected. But at that time, they were not very strict on the selection because they told the children that-- that the children are being selected to be taken to a children camp who are getting much better conditions. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But most of the children there were naive, so we'll just-- they didn't need much encouragement to go the children camp. But I didn't want to go to the children camp because I-- I knew what-- I had a-- I knew what it mean, children camp. So it was not difficult to-- getting-- getting out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you know what it meant? From what people had told you, or it was just an instinct that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: What people-- what people told me. And I had an instinct that-- that this is-- that this is-- the Germans are not making for the children a special treatment. And when they take children, it's because they are useless for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was something about your personality that enabled you to-- to suss it out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly, because-- because-- a lot of-- a lot of people even older than me, and they just went like the sheep to-- to-- to the selections. And they didn't-- they didn't fought-- they haven't fought to get out of it. I-- I-- I fought. I thought this is only way of survival, not-- not-- not to getting selected. To-- and-- and I managed to avoid this successfully. I mean, it's really a miracle how I avoided it a second time and a third time, because I was always being taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you avoid it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was running all the time. Then the second section was-- was again, the day before Yom Kippur. The Germans selected these days for the selection. And the-- the whole camp was-- was one big selection. Everybody in the camp had to go to the large-- large square of the camp. And they put up a-- a piece of timber horizontally. And people who didn't reach that height, they have to put on one side. But as soon as I was put on the wrong side, I immediately jumped over to those-- to those that were selected that they are all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nobody saw you do it, or nobody stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They saw me do it. And-- and-- and-- they-- and I started running. And somebody run after me, one of the-- one of the kapos, which is-- and as he caught up with me, he says to me in Yiddish, he says, he says, run away from me. That's what I did. So he pretended that he's holding me. And he said to me that I should-- I should free myself from him and run away. And I-- I-- I ran and ran. And then-- then the German also was a prisoner, but they were just as wicked as the SS. He-- he notices that I escaped from this Jewish kapo or something. And he run after me. And luckily, at that day, there was-- there was a foggy day. And in a fog, the [INAUDIBLE] commanders, those people are going-- going out to work. They are not going to work because they are-- they are worried about escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they stayed in the camp. And there was a lot of-- a lot of Russian prisoners of war in the camp. They were just strong, grown up people. And they stopped [NON-ENGLISH]. Stop, don't go after him. And it looked like he was afraid of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he let me run. So I run away. That was-- that was my second escape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and still on the same selection to the next day--

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day there was another selection? The same selection. Right, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, same selection, next day, Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on Yom Kippur. That-- on-- on the night of-- on night of [INAUDIBLE], they-- they are from the barracks, which were the children who were selected. Somehow, they broke out, and there was 20 children disappeared from the camp-- from that-- from that closed barrack. And they dispersed in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, when they made the countings, the appel, which was a daily routine in the camp, they had to fill in-- they had to find 20-- 20 or so people. And because, you see, the Germans-- everything-- once they had been selected, they said, how many people have been selected? And they have to keep the count of these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and the block commander, who was in the barrack, he is responsible that-- should-- should-- for the numbers. So when they came to the appel, I was standing-- we were standing in rows of five, and I was standing on a piece of brick or something that I should look-- I-- I shouldn't look so auspicious that I'm-- I'm small. And I should look like the same, like the others. But when the SS man came and he saw me, he just picked me out. He just pulled me out and says, you go. You go to the other barracks. I knew what he want. And I started crying-- crying to him that I-- I'm strong. I can work. And there was-- there was a German prisoner, that lageralteste, and he gave me a smack in the face and said, shut up, in German. And the-- and the-- and the SS man said, ah, [INAUDIBLE] him. Leave him alone. So he pushed me back, and he took someone next to me, just like that. That was between life and death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was another selection. That, I don't remember how I escaped. But by that-- by that time, I had experience how to always make myself-- whenever there is-- there is-- Mengele came into the camp, I just-- one thing one thing you have to know, that-- that when they make the selection, then-- then you don't-- you don't count the numbers that-- numbers that everybody is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you are hiding, and you don't appear at the selection, then you can avoid it. That's what I learned by the third selection. So I survived. I survived all the three selections. And do you think that because of your age and-- and size, you would have otherwise? Inevitably--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would-- I-- yeah, no doubt about it. No doubt about it. I would not-- in their eyes, I was not fit to work-- 14-year-old small, little boy. It's-- it's-- it's not an-- not an asset for the German war machine, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- all the time was-- was-- was-- in fact-- in fact, what happened, I just found out later. We had a friend, also from the same town as we are, who managed to work in the kitchen-- on the-- on the kitchen of the camp. And he managed to smuggle us out some extra food almost daily.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother-- my brother found out that I'd been selected. He didn't know that I managed immediately to escape from the selection. He went to see, look, what can we do? Because you know, you could bribe-- bribe the-- the-- the block commanders who are in charge with something, some people with dollars, or jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or even-- even in my case, my friend in the kitchen bribed him with-- with three bales of potatoes that he should release me. And he gave-- he took-- he took him the potatoes. But I was not in his-- in his barrack, because I managed to get-- I-- I managed to avoid it. But he told me now that he got three-- because I met him. Just this-- just this summer, I met this guy. He lives in New York. And he told me-- he told me the story, that he-- that he paid me, for my life, three bales of potatoes from the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd already gone? You'd already escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I already-- yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that Mengele kind of-- you had this contact with him right on the-- the very beginning. Did you see him frequently afterwards? Did he know again who you-- did he seem to know who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think he knew me. I know him. He didn't-- he didn't know me, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't remember from that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I mean, he must have passed hundreds of thousands of people through his life. I mean, a human life, in his eyes, was just a flicker of the finger. And he decide on the spot-- on the spot, within seconds of human life, just-- just like that, pointing-- pointing left or right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- I mean, how did you know-- what did you know about Mengele at the time? I mean, you just knew he was a powerful man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a powerful man. He was called the lager doctor, the lagerarzt. And also I knew about it, that he's carrying out-- I knew about his-- that he's-- he's-- he's doing medical experiment work somewhere in his-- in the laboratory. And we knew that-- that twins, that was his favorite subject of research. And they had-- they had-- they lived in a separate barracks. And they had somewhat better treatment. Possibly, they want to keep-- keep them in a better physical condition for his experiments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were envying the twins, that they looked like they were always getting more food. But at the end of the day, not many of them survived. I mean, a lot has been written about Mengele-- Mengele's experiments. And I can only say-- I cannot say, only what I heard. So I-- I just-- it's not really relevant what I-- what I-- what I thought. And possibly, this is what I know about it. I just read about it later on. At that time, we didn't know anything about it. I just knew something that-- something fishy going on with the twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also said that the selections were on Erev Rosh Hashanah and Erev Yom Kippur. Now, how did you-- how did you know when the Jewish-- I mean, did you have a sense of time and-- and dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew-- we knew. No, we had-- we had-- funny enough, there was always somebody in the camp that knew about-- about Jewishness and knew about the-- when is-- when is the dates. In fact, my brother had a-- a-- a [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish prayer book for the festivals. He paid for it a whole bread. And we had a [NON-ENGLISH], one set of [NON-ENGLISH] we had in the camp. And we put on [NON-ENGLISH] every day. Whenever-- I wouldn't say all the time, but while we had access to the pair of [NON-ENGLISH], there was-- it was queuing up from the early morning till-- till late. And everybody just had to put on their hand [NON-ENGLISH] and their hat [NON-ENGLISH] and just have to say the first-- the first chapter of Shema and the next one. And they were queuing up hundreds of people for that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did you do to maintain your-- your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't have to do a lot with the Jewish identity as such, because we were Jewish. We were Jewish. But I mean, why--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Jewish life, rather than your Jewish identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life. I mean, I-- I-- I was eating whatever-- whatever is provided. I-- I-- and-- and sometimes-- sometimes, you know, in the evening, with the bread, occasionally, you-- you had a bit of-- bit of-- piece of salami or-- or something. My brother didn't eat it. But I-- I-- I ate it. I-- I thought I have to. Right. And most important, I realize, is that we are-- we are now in exceptional-- exceptional times. And the main aim in life now is to survive. And anything else would be subordinate to that. And to be surviving, not only-- not only-- not only forget about Jewish practice, but also forget about that you are a human being. Anything-- anything which-- which matters in life, if you-- if you start thinking what you are, what happened, then you will just go and electrocute yourself to the-- to the electric wire. But I thought, I mustn't think about it. I just has to behave like an animal. Just-- just survival. Survival to avoid being-- being beaten up, avoid-- try to get as much food like as-- as you can, and just survival. And everything spiritual comes if you manage to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had-- I had some great doubts about it at end of the day. I-- I will survive. Even after when I survived the selections. I remember, because later on, after the selection period, which was those-- the time of the Jewish festivals, coincided with the Jewish festivals. Not-- not by-- not by just coincidence, the Germans made it especially to-- to hurt the Jews even more. But afterwards, they stopped the-- stopped the selections in Auschwitz. Stopped the-- the gassing. It came an order from Himmler. And in fact, in fact, I was there in the camp when people were already in the gas chambers, and there came the order that they should-- that they-- that the gassing has to stop. They were told, dress again, and-- and-- and leave the camp. I was in the camp when-- when people came back from the gas chambers. It never happened before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they thought they were going to their-- to their death, and they walked about again alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and they-- and they had an order that the gassing has to stop. And the Germans are following orders, so they immediately stopped the gassing. They took out a group of youngsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you understand by that, that the gassing was stopped-- was to stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, what-- what I understood at that time, that the Germans, they changed their policies. The reason is because-- because by that time, it must have been October or November, they-- they realized that Auschwitz has to be evacuated very soon because the Russians are near-- getting near. And they have to-- have to avoid-- have to avoid all the-- the signs or the traces of what happened here. In fact, that was only-- only bit of work which I-- which I carried out in my camp. I was not-- I was never fit to work. But-- but when-- when the gas chambers were-- were destroyed, were-- were demolished, I was working to take away the rubble with carts from the place where the gas chamber [INAUDIBLE] to somewhere else in the open. That was a bit of work which I did. But it was a good sign that there-- there is no gassing. So-- so that danger is off. But how I'm going to survive to the last, when Auschwitz is going to be evacuated, that was still very much in-- in doubt, very much in question.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't work most of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you occupy yourself? What did-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do? I-- I don't remember. Well, just-- just-- there was always highlights of the day. There's three times when we had other-- other liquids at breakfast and-- and-- and-- and cooked-- cooked something, and in the evening. And there were always appels in between. I was coming and going in the camp, visiting here and there when-- when-- when it was-- I was not doing anything specific.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the other people in your-- in your barracks were people-- were teenagers who were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in my barracks-- in my barracks, where I was, nobody was working. It wasn't-- it was not a working barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, after the selections, then-- then-- then they said that everybody has to get some kind of work. So I would-- I would also be-- be allocated to some commander. That was the time when I was working on the-- on the-- the removal of the-- of the rubble of the crematoriums and the gas chambers. That was the only work. And then I became ill suddenly, end of-- end of November. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was taken to hospital, in-- in Auschwitz hospital. Auschwitz-- rather, Birkenau, had a hospital. This is-- this is a great contrast what with the Germans and their devilish ways. Even-- even-- in fact, the-- the-- the hospital camp-- it was a separate enclosure camp, there were not many barracks there-- was very near to the gas chambers. And-- and-- the-- and all kind of illnesses were treated there in the hospital. There was Jewish doctors. There was no shortage of medicine because of the transport brought everything. And the-- and the-- there were hospitals. So if a-- so if a-- if a Swiss Cross-- a-- a-- a Red Cross delegation would come to Auschwitz, they would just show them, look here, we are treating-- we've got excellent hospitals here. But what-- what the truth was, the hospitals, once it became full, they emptied it out. Everybody. They went to the gas chambers. And the next day, they started admission again. That's how it works, the hospital in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you were ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I was ill. I was taken to hospital. I had typhoid. I didn't know what I had. All I know, that I have very high temperature. I couldn't stand on my feet. And-- and I knew that-- that physically, I-- I-- I knew that some kind of illness I have got.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me in. They made all kind of tests. And by the time it came back, the test that I-- that I had typhoid, I was already recovered. Just by your own bodily--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my own body resistance. I-- I recovered. In the hospital-- in fact, you had a little bit more food than in the camp. And I recovered-- not only that I recovered. They took from the blood, I think it was 100 centiliter of blood they took for me, for using it for a serum. And they took my name. And a week later, a German SS man came into the hospital and ask for my name. And I got very frightened. I see a general officer asked specially for my name. And he got for me a [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE], because you gave the blood, you gave. I mean, I didn't give them. They took it from me. But, nevertheless, it came handy to me. So these kind of incidents, sometimes you see from the Germans such a contrast. And one day, one day, they heartlessly, without emotion, they are killing hundreds of thousands of people. And then they make exceptions. They make-- make something like that. I-- I really don't know. Anyway, I-- I-- I didn't philosophize a lot into it. I just ate it and finish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because the hospital was not full at that time, you were able to leave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't-- you said the others got taken to-- to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was there-- I recovered there, and I-- and I stayed in the hospital. And it was a very good-- I-- I-- I start-- I was hoping that I'm going to pull through the whole winter in the hospital because it was more pleasant than to go out to work in the cold. And by the-- by January, that's January of 1945, then we-- then we heard that-- that the Russians are getting near, and Auschwitz will be evacuated very soon. We had to-- they will take us somewhere further into Germany. And I asked the hospital that. I want to go-- I don't want to stay in the hospital anymore. Because I felt-- I felt if this evacuation is going to come, maybe they don't bother with the-- with the-- with the people who are in the hospital, the patients. They will kill them on the spot. And only they will march those people, who are getting ready to march.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I want to get out of the hospital, which he let me. He-- he didn't mind. He-- I could have stayed if I want to. But I rather-- I rather-- my own request, I ask for-- for to be released from the hospital. So were there about four or five weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was there exactly, if you wish to know, on the-- from the 24th of November-- November [INAUDIBLE], through the 16th of January, I was there in the hospital, which was-- I really recovered. It was a good-- a better condition, the hospital, than in the camp. It was quite comparatively, it was good for me that I stayed there. And 16th of January, I went back to the camp. I hardly found my allocated barrack. Two days later, we were evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that evacuation and how that happened, I want to ask you a bit about whether you made relationships with people in the-- in the concentration camp in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had-- I had friends. Yes, I do. And I even got-- even know people whom I were together with them, even up to that very day. For instance, I know-- I know brothers who one of them was selected to the gas chambers. And-- and-- and he came back. He came back when the gassing was stopped. And both of them, they live in London. And I-- I-- I know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- they provided-- you provided sort of support for each other [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mean, that's what the gassing that my brother-- older brother was with me, and that friend of mine from-- who worked in the kitchen. But, really, there was no such a thing as social life in the camp. It's everybody was for himself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you haven't seen many acts of kindness or-- Very occasionally, occasionally you could see something. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But generally speaking, I cannot speak much-- much of-- much of spiritual life or social life into the camp. I mean, I mean, the Germans had succeeded to bring us down to the level of animals. And most of the people behaved like animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you see some behavior amongst people that you-- that really quite shocked you in some ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's-- it's-- it's one of the questions like that. It was not-- it was not a question of-- of-- of helping others or-- or-- everybody wants to-- wants to bother with himself. Everybody became very selfish. You had to be selfish to survive. I mean, I-- I know, for instance, I-- I was a bit more selfish than my old-- my brother. My brother, one-- once, I remember, in the summer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a boy of my age was crying in the morning that he had his-- he-- he was stolen from him his bread. He hasn't ate up his bread portion. He put it away for the morning. And it was stolen from him during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother gave him a piece of his own ration. I mean, this is something which I-- I didn't understand. I-- [LAUGHS] but he-- he's maybe the higher spiritual level than I am. From his own-- and he was so hungry. He was so thin, because he was taller than me. He needed-- he needed more food than I-- than I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, nevertheless, he-- he-- he-- he really-- really-- I'm looking up to him. That-- that with the-- that with the bread. And then when-- when he-- for a-- for a bread from his rations, put away a whole bread to buy a prayer book for-- for Rosh Hashanah. This is something. He lives here in London. He's older than me. He's got--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you respect him for those things that he was doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- do, I do up to very day. Up to very day. He-- he had more spiritual life in me-- than in me. I-- it looks like I was more-- more practical and more selfish. I remember, I ask him-- I ask him, why are-- so-- so you don't say the whole prayers. You will-- you will say as much as you remember. But you give away a whole bread? It's-- it's-- it's overpaid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older than you was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two and a half years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was also somewhere in the camp but you never saw her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sister, we didn't know. We didn't know anything about my sister, because-- because the other day, when we arrived in Auschwitz, she went in the-- in the women's camp, and that was the last we heard of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the last you saw of her.

words.text: the last you saw of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, what we-- first, we knew about her [INAUDIBLE] after the war, when we found out that she is in Germany, and soon we will-- we will-- she will be back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move on to your evacuation, is there anything more about the Auschwitz experiences that you feel you want to-- to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there were a lot of-- lot of little incidents which I don't remember clearly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, I don't think-- I don't think it just comes to me anything else. Maybe I should have-- should have prepared myself better for the interview, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] 12 minutes. So how did the-- what happened at the-- back when you were evacuated?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were evacuated, it was-- it was early morning. They said we are going to be evacuated, and we had to stand up. But everybody had to-- had to stand up in rounds of five. And just you see that-- that-- that the beginning of the [INAUDIBLE] is already out of the gates of the camp, and we're just following it. It was quite-- quite in a rush. And we just walked, kept on walking, accompanied-- accompanied by Wehrmacht soldiers. Possibly, they couldn't spare the SS by then. And we walked for-- for almost 24 hours. And it was very hard for me, because-- because I just came out from the hospital. Besides, even if I would have been my normal strength, but being in hospital, when you had no-- no chance of walking or exercise. And your body gets a bit flabby. And after-- after the night's walking, I just couldn't-- couldn't-- couldn't walk any further. Then-- then they said, since people started struggling behind. And then they said, people who cannot walk any further should stand-- should step out of the line, should stand aside. And then I realized that this is end now. This is end is coming. There is-- but I couldn't walk any further. My-- my-- my body has really given up. I-- I just-- I just haven't had the energy. My feet were swollen. I've discarded the blanket that they had given us in the-- in the beginning of the march. I just walked as a robot. But just-- I found myself that I cannot walk any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stepped aside, and I was prepared for the-- for the worst. That I realized that-- that I'm not going to-- to leave here. But, fortunately, this is not what happened. We were taken into another camp.
